forecasts.reasoning:
The total time from the recall being issued (April 5, 2025) to the resolution date (July 1, 2025) is only 86 days. The USDA FSIS recall closure process is notoriously slow, typically taking 6 to 12 months (or more). A recall is only 'Closed' after all reasonable efforts to retrieve and dispose of the product are completed, the recalling firm submits its final documentation, FSIS conducts effectiveness checks, RMTAD evaluates the findings, and the administrative status on the public website is finally updated. 

With 22,000 pounds of product likely distributed to multiple retail locations, it will take several weeks just to manage the retrieval and consumer notifications. Even if the product has a short refrigerated shelf life, the administrative delay in verifying disposal and formally updating the FSIS website nearly always exceeds the 86-day window available. If the product can be frozen, the closure timeline would be extended even further. Therefore, it is highly unlikely to be marked as 'Closed' before July 1, 2025.

The forecast horizon is extremely short (May 9, 2025 to July 1, 2025, or 52 days). GoPro has had structural challenges with hardware sales but possesses a profitable subscription segment and historically maintained an adequate cash pile to avoid acute, sudden liquidity crises. Their primary upcoming debt maturity is the 2025 convertible senior notes, which were issued in November 2020 and likely mature in November 2025 -- well after the July 1 deadline. Standard base rates for a mid-cap public company abruptly filing for bankruptcy within a strict 52-day window without an imminent forcing mechanism (like a debt cliff) are extremely low. Given the lack of specific evidence of acute distress (e.g., hiring restructuring advisors), I am heavily weighting the base rate.

The word 'tariff' is extremely unlikely to appear on the front page of AT LEAST one of these two papers every single day for months on end. Newspaper front pages regularly shift focus due to breaking news, international crises, or weekend feature stories. Furthermore, the WSJ does not publish a Sunday print edition, meaning Sunday omissions only depend on the NYT front page omitting the word. By May 9, 2025, it is essentially certain that there has already been at least one day where neither paper mentioned 'tariff' on its front page, fulfilling the YES criteria.
